note:
上周，/r/DataHoarder的优秀Rededers再次对西部数据感到不安-这一次，是因为他们歪曲了WD Red Network Attached Storage硬盘的转速。(尽管这篇链接的帖子把事情带到了紧要关头，德语论坛hardwareluxx.de的成员在一年多前就开始调查这个问题了。)。
我们发现，这场争议让人想起了早先的抱怨，即西部数据没有正确披露在其NAS驱动器中使用瓦片磁记录技术的情况。但新的抱怨是，西部数据称7200RPM驱动器为5400RPM级，而这些驱动器通过智能界面拥有5400RPM的固件报告。
最近，redditor/u/amaroko开始证明或反驳早先的网民的发现。对于几种驱动器型号中的每一种，Amoroko都将该驱动器的样本放在一个空纸箱上，正上方举着一个蓝色雪人麦克风，然后打开驱动器的电源。使用Adobe Audition对录制的音频进行频谱分析显示，两种型号的WD8TB；5400转/分驱动器的基准频率为120 Hz。
120周期/秒乘以60秒/分钟等于7,200周期/分钟。换句话说，这些5400转/分的驱动器真的是以7200转/分的速度旋转。
乍一看，这似乎不是什么问题--谁不喜欢磁盘轴速度更快的驱动器呢？不幸的是，更快的磁盘轴不仅意味着潜在的更低的寻道延迟-它们还会带来噪音产生和功耗的急剧增加。
噪音和功率的增加让许多用户一开始就跟踪西部数据的假5400rpm主轴速度-这些用户购买了他们预计会低速滚动的驱动器，但他们得到的噪音、热量和功耗比他们预期的要高。
通过比较5,400转/分的希捷梭鱼和5,400转/分的西部数字红色(经音频频谱分析测试，实际转速为7,200转/分)的数据表，我们可以看到明显的功耗差异。8TB红色在激活时消耗8.8瓦到梭鱼的5.3瓦，在待机模式下消耗800兆瓦到梭鱼的250兆瓦。这里的区别在很大程度上不在于品牌-当它们以相同的实际转速旋转时，西部数据和希捷的驱动器消耗的功率大致相同。
公平地说，你根本买不到转速为5400rpm的8TB NAS驱动器--WD的Red和希捷的Ironwolf入门级NAS产品都使用7200rpm的磁盘轴。但公平地说，对消费大众来说，转速有一个非常具体的含义，WD Red数据表上甚至没有脚注来解释5400转/分性能等级的实际含义(如果有的话)。
当我们在研究这篇报道的过程中联系西部数据时，一位代表证实了参加论坛的各种人和Redditor的结论-也就是说，5400转/分的等级实际上并不意味着驱动器以5400转/分的速度旋转。
对于精选的产品，西部数据多年来一直在“级别”或“性能级别”内公布转速，而不是公布具体的主轴速度。我们还对精选的硬盘平台和相关的硬盘特性进行了微调，以创建几种不同的此类平台，以满足不同的市场或应用需求。通过这样做，我们能够利用我们的规模经济，并将这些节省下来的成本传递给我们的客户。与西部数据的每一种产品一样，我们的产品细节(包括功率、声学和性能(数据传输率))都经过测试，以满足产品数据表和市场宣传资料中提供的规格。
在我们的社论观点中，这种回应，无论多么完美，都无济于事--仍然没有定义的RPM课程本身也是如此。阅读这份数据表的消费者将更多地被营销的无稽之谈所迷惑，而不是更少。可以想象，西部数据试图屏蔽他们不理解的信息的典型消费者，从一开始就不太可能阅读硬盘数据手册。
如果你原谅一个汽车的类比，这与V6轿车上的车窗贴纸没有太大不同，上面写着它是四缸级的，但没有说明引擎盖下到底是什么。“。
note_en:
Last week, the fine Redditors of /r/DataHoarder got upset with Western Digital  again—this time, for misrepresenting the rotational speed of their WD Red Network Attached Storage hard drives. (Although the linked post brings things to a head, members of the German-language forum hardwareluxx.de began  investigating the issue more than year ago.)
 We found this controversy reminiscent of  earlier complaints that Western Digital was not properly disclosing use of Shingled Magnetic Recording technology in their NAS drives. But the new complaint is that Western Digital calls 7200RPM drives "5400 RPM Class"—and the drives' own firmware report 5400 RPM via the SMART interface.
 Recently, redditor /u/Amaroko set out to prove or disprove earlier netizens' findings. For each of several drive models, Amoroko placed a sample of that drive on an empty cardboard box, with a Blue Yeti mic held directly above it, then powered the drive on. Spectral analysis of the recorded audio using Adobe Audition showed a baseline frequency of 120Hz for two models of WD 8TB "5400 RPM class" drive.
 120 cycles/sec multiplied to 60 secs/min comes to 7,200 cycles/min. So in other words, these "5400 RPM class" drives really were spinning at 7,200rpm.
   At first blush, this might seem like a non-issue—who wouldn't prefer a drive with a faster spindle speed? Unfortunately, faster spindles don't just mean potentially lower seek latency—they also come with a sharp increase in both noise generation and power consumption.
 That increase in noise and power is what got many users on the trail of Western Digital's fake 5,400rpm spindle speed in the first place—those users purchased drives which they expected to roll low and slow, but they got more noise, heat, and power consumption than they expected.
 Comparing the data sheets between a 5,400rpm Seagate Barracuda and a "5400 RPM Class" Western Digital Red—which, as tested by audio spectral analysis, actually spins at 7,200rpm—we can see a clear difference in power consumption. The 8TB Red consumes 8.8W to the Barracuda's 5.3W when active, and 800mW to the Barracuda's 250mW when in standby mode. The difference here isn't, for the most part, the brand—when they're spinning at the same actual RPMs, Western Digital's and Seagate's drives consume roughly the same power.
 To be fair to Western Digital, you can't actually buy an 8TB NAS drive that spins at 5,400RPM at all—both WD's Red and Seagate's Ironwolf entry-level NAS lineup use 7,200rpm spindles. But to be fair to the consuming public, "RPM" has a very specific meaning, and there isn't so much as a footnote on the WD Red  datasheet to explain what—if anything—"5400 RPM performance class" actually means.
  When we reached out to Western Digital in the course of researching this story, a representative confirmed the various forum-goers' and Redditor's conclusions—that is to say, "5400 RPM class" does not actually mean that a drive spins at 5,400rpm.
 For select products, Western Digital has published RPM speed within a “class” or “performance class” for numerous years rather than publishing specific spindle speeds. We also fine-tune select hard drive platforms and the related HDD characteristics to create several different variations of such platforms to meet different market or application needs. By doing so, we are able to leverage our economies of scale and pass along those savings to our customers. As with every Western Digital product, our product details, which include power, acoustics and performance (data transfer rate), are tested to meet the specifications provided on the product’s data sheet and marketing collateral.
 In our editorial opinion, this response, however polished, is not helpful—and neither is the still-undefined "RPM class" itself. A consumer reading this data sheet will be  more confused by the marketing fluff, not less. And the "typical consumers" who Western Digital might conceivably be trying to shield from information they don't understand are unlikely to be reading hard drive data sheets in the first place.
 If you'll pardon an automotive analogy, this doesn't strike us as much different than a window sticker on a V6 sedan which declares it to be "four cylinder class," with no indication of what's actually under the hood.
title: 西部数据试图重新定义“RPM”这个词
title_en: Western Digital is trying to redefine the word “RPM”

note:
这是一种在时尚和配饰方面效果很好的模式(只要问问Rent the Runway的投资者就知道了)，但在白标商品方面却没有得到同样的共鸣。
由长期担任营销高管的首席执行官凯伦·罗杰斯·奥尼尔(Karen Rodgers O‘Neil)和连续创业和技术高管丹尼尔·佩罗内(Daniel Perrone)发起；佩罗内之前的公司BroadMap被苹果收购；该棚希望将家得宝(Home Depot)变成10亿美元业务线的租赁模式带给大众。
与家得宝(Home Depot)不同，这个棚子吹嘘自己在八个类别中的存在。斯坦利·布莱克·德克尔(Stanley Black&amp；Decker)是该公司重要的早期合伙人，该公司高管表示，其他公司也加入了进来。
“我们不买产品，”佩龙说。“我们接受所有产品的交货，然后在我们开展业务的当地市场出租。”
制造商唯一提供的是产品和一些维修入门套件，以便机棚及其员工可以管理和维护产品。
自4月份成立以来，该公司已经扩展到弗吉尼亚州里士满以外的地区。总部设在丹佛-根据佩隆的说法，他将寻求进一步扩展到波特兰、奥斯汀和圣何塞。
该公司打算在扩张过程中推出的功能之一是动态定价功能，这将使制造商在需求旺盛时能够最大限度地利用他们的产品。
罗杰斯·奥尼尔早在2012年就提出了这个概念，当时她正在波士顿担任通用电气(General Electric)的营销主管。佩罗内在波士顿的一次社交活动上遇到了罗杰斯·奥尼尔，并确信她提供更多租赁选择以鼓励更循环的经济和减少消费的想法可能会引起消费者的共鸣。
可以肯定的是，小屋并不是第一家试图将租赁业务引入更广泛的消费产品以努力减少消费的公司。总部位于洛杉矶的初创公司Joymode也试图做同样的事情。那家公司卖给了纽约外的一家早期投资公司。
Joymode的首席执行官乔·费尔南德斯(Joe Fernandez)谈到了经营该业务的困难。费尔南德斯说：“论文的一部分是，通过提供出租服务，人们会想要做更多的事情。”但事实是，消费者需要更多的理由才能使用公司的服务，而没有足够的活动来推动需求。
相比之下，仓库并不拥有任何库存，只是充当当地零售商和制造商之间的经纪人和管理库存，这些零售商和制造商希望利用公司的服务。
除了斯坦利·布莱克(Stanley Black&amp；Decker)，像Primus露营设备这样的公司已经把他们的产品放在棚子里，还有Mobility Plus，它增加了轮椅和移动滑板车；Replacements，美国最大的瓷器经销商，提供晚餐、鸡尾酒或茶会的“盒子里的派对”。
到目前为止，该公司已经从弗吉尼亚州里士满的投资者和企业家那里筹集了175万美元。区域。现在，有60家制造商在船上，每月还有15到18家供应商签约，该公司正寻求进一步扩张。
佩隆说：“我加入凯伦是因为我看到这将改变租赁领域的游戏规则。”他说，有一些特定垂直领域的零售商仍然不在网上交易，所以棚子成为他们进入市场的渠道。
note_en:
It’s a model that’s worked famously well for fashion and accessories ( just ask investors in Rent the Runway), but has had not had the same resonance for white label goods.
   Launched by  Karen Rodgers O’Neil,  a longtime marketing executive, and Daniel Perrone, a serial entrepreneur and technology executive whose previous company, BroadMap, was acquired by Apple; The Shed hopes to take the rental model that Home Depot has turned into a billion dollar business line and take it to the masses.
 Unlike Home Depot, The Shed touts its presence in eight categories. Stanley Black & Decker is a marquee early partner and the company’s executives said that others have come on board.
 “We don’t buy product,” said Perrone. “We take delivery of all the products and rent them out in the local marketplaces where we do business.”
 The only thing the manufacturer provides is the products and some servicing starter kit so that The Shed and its employees can manage and maintain the product.
  Since its launch in April the company has expanded beyond its Richmond, Va. home base to Denver — and will be looking to expand further into Portland, Austin, and San Jose, according to Perrone.
 Among the features that the company intends to roll out as it expands is a dynamic pricing capability that will enable manufacturers to wring the most out of their goods when they’re in high demand.
 Rodgers O’Neil came up with the concept back in 2012 when she was working as a marketing executive for General Electric out of Boston.  Perrone met Rodgers O’Neil at a networking event in Boston and became convinced that her notion of offering more rental options to encourage a more circular economy and reduce consumption was something that could resonate with consumers.
 To be sure, The Shed isn’t the first company to attempt to bring the rental business to a broader array of consumer products in an effort to cut down on consumption. The Los Angeles-based startup  Joymode   was attempting to do much the same thing. That company sold to an early stage investment firm out of New York.
 Joymode’s chief executive,  Joe Fernandez   spoke about the difficulty of running the business. “Part of the thesis was that by making things available for rental, people would want to do more stuff,” said Fernandez, but what happened was that consumers needed additional reasons to use the company’s service, and there weren’t enough events to drive demand.
  By contrast, The Shed isn’t owning any of the inventory, just acting as a broker and managing inventory between local retailers and manufacturers who want to take advantage of the company’s service.
 In addition to Stanley Black & Decker, companies like Primus camping equipment have placed their products on The Shed along with Mobility Plus, which added wheelchairs and mobility scooters; and Replacements, the largest china dealer in the country, which is offering a “Party in a Box” for dinner, cocktail or tea parties.
 To date, the company has raised $1.75 million from investors and entrepreneurs from the Richmond, Va. area. Now, with 60 manufacturers on board and another 15 to 18 vendors signing up monthly, the company is looking to expand even further.
 “I joined with Karen because I saw that this would be a game changer in the rental space,” said Perrone. There are a number of retailers in specific verticals that still don’t transact online, so The Shed becomes their avenue to reach the market, he said.

成交，我们成交了。本周，一个新的SPAC脱颖而出。是的，你知道的。我就知道；我们都知道另一个SPAC要来了。SPAC的一些合并声明让人感觉像是年轻的未经证实的公司为获得资本而不顾一切的尝试。本周情况并非如此。
Quantumscape并不是一家羽翼未丰的初创公司。它已经存在了十年，很早就吸引了凯鹏华盈(Kleiner Perkins)和科斯拉风险投资公司(Khosla Ventures)等知名风险投资公司的关注和资金。大众汽车于2012年进入这一领域，目前已向Quantumscape投资总计3亿美元，其中包括今年的2亿美元。
Quantumscape正在追求资本密集型的目标，即试图将电动汽车的固态电池商业化。固态电池使用固体电解质，而不是锂离子电池中的液体或凝胶电解质。开发人员声称，固体电解质具有更高的能量密度，这转化为从更小、更轻的电池中挤压出更多的续航里程。固体电解质还应该在热管理方面做得更好，减少火灾风险，并减少对当今电动汽车中各种冷却系统的依赖。

Hello and welcome back to The Station, a newsletter dedicated to all the present and future ways people and packages move from Point A to Point B.
 As summer comes to an end, deals have lagged a skosh ahead of what promises to be a busy fall. And while the news cycle continues, there has been a slight dip in intensity. Sounds like a good time to take a break, no? Yup, it is. Next week, there will not be an issue of the newsletter. Don’t worry, it will return Sept. 19.
  Email me anytime at  kirsten.korosec@techcrunch.com to share thoughts, criticisms, offer up opinions or tips. You can also send a direct message to me at Twitter —  @kirstenkorosec.
    Deals, we got em. And this week, a new SPAC stands out. Yup, you knew it. I knew it; we all knew another SPAC was coming. Some SPAC merger announcements feel like a desperate attempt by young unproven companies to access capital. That’s not the case this week.
  QuantumScape is not a fledgling startup. It’s been around for decade, attracting attention and capital early on from high-profile venture firms like Kleiner Perkins  and Khosla Ventures. Volkswagen entered the picture in 2012 and has invested a total of $300 million in QuantumScape, including $200 million this year.
 QuantumScape is going after the capitally intensive goal of attempting to commercialize solid-state batteries for electric vehicles. Solid-state batteries use a solid electrolyte and not a liquid or gel-based electrolyte found in lithium-ion batteries. Developers claim that solid electrolytes have greater energy density, which translates into squeezing more range out of a smaller and lighter battery. Solid electrolytes also are supposed to be better at thermal management, reducing the risk of fire and the reliance on the kinds of cooling systems found in today’s EVs.

note:
Mollie是一家初创公司，提供一种简单的、基于API的方式将支付整合到网站或应用程序中，该公司在TCV牵头的一轮融资中筹集了9000万欧元(1.06亿美元)。创始人兼首席执行官阿德里安·莫尔在接受TechCrunch采访时证实，B系列使Mollie筹集的资金总额达到1.15亿欧元，值得注意的是，这家初创公司的估值超过10亿美元。
Mollie自2004年以来一直存在，这只是它第二次筹集资金-第一次是一年前的2500万欧元-这可能是它没有太多受到创业公司关注的原因之一。
“当我还和父母住在一起的时候，它自己建造了后端和前端，”莫尔说。“这是荷兰人的方式。在相当长的一段时间内引导你的想法。我认为这是公司的基础。“。
然而，莫莉在过去几年里达到了一些里程碑，这为它为什么现在以如此高的估值出现在舞台上提供了一些解释，似乎是凭空而来的。
目前，该公司主要专注于中小企业-一个服务严重不足但巨大的市场-客户约10万家，主要在荷兰、比利时和德国。这些客户包括一些非常知名的公司，如Wickey、Deliveroo、TOMS(鞋类公司)和联合国儿童基金会。
目前，该公司今年的交易额有望超过100亿欧元，同比增长100%，德国等一些市场的交易额增长了1000%。在这一点上，它已经盈利了很多年。
“我们在效率方面是正确的，”莫尔说--莫莉是他在朋友中的昵称。“但我们需要投资新产品以保持竞争力。”
事实上，支付服务提供商的市场相当拥挤，像斯利普和莫莉的同胞阿迪恩这样的公司也凭借提供API和几行简单的代码来将支付流整合到其他服务的概念上建立了强大的业务。这些公司不仅很受欢迎，而且资本雄厚，随时准备继续开发更多工具，并简单地继续扩大其国际足迹。
莫尔说，莫莉在两个关键方面与他们和其他竞争对手脱颖而出。首先，它在一个仍然非常分散的市场上提供非常本地化的支付服务，每个消费者和商家对使用哪种支付方式的偏好在不同的国家有很大不同(仍然没有完全得到Strip这样的公司的服务)。
第二，它提供了一个非常快速和简单的集成，隐藏了集成这么多不同类型的支付方式的许多困难。商家和客户的易用性意味着更少的购物车被遗弃，因此网站访问者的转换率更高。Mol说，转换率通常可能高达7%。
他说：“如果旅程非常顺畅，他们就不会退出，这就是我们客户的直接收入。”(定价是透明的，但不是统一的：它取决于数量，以及整合和使用了哪些支付方式。)。
这一点-加上一家非常有利可图、效率很高的公司的增长率-是吸引TCV的一部分。
“易用性真的很关键，”TCV合伙人约翰·多兰(John Doran)说。“这太简单了，小孩子都能用。我们的想法是打造支付界的苹果。
Mol第一次有构建Mollie的想法是在他将一项支付服务集成到他之前的初创公司MessageBird中时，MessageBird提供基于API的消息服务(可以将其视为欧洲版的Twilio)。当时，他说他发现所有可用的支付产品都低于标准，太难按照他想要的方式使用，所以他们-特别是莫尔自己，他是一名工程师-内部构建了解决方案(字面意思是，他父母的房子：见上图)。
我问该公司为什么不简单地进行扩张和/或转向，在一个保护伞下提供这两项服务，但莫尔表示，这两项业务和想法太大了，而且不够相似，不足以发展为一项业务。因此，他离开了MessageBird的高管领导层-尽管他仍在董事会-全职关注莫莉。
Mol表示，该公司的下一步将是继续建设支付附近的服务-比如可能为客户提供营运资金的领域，以及中小企业通常不会从单一提供商那里获得的其他金融服务。“我们可以看到一大堆传统银行没有提供给中小企业的产品，”莫尔说。“他们没有动力去投资，因为他们从中赚不到多少钱。”他补充说，这些可能包括销售点支付、信用卡发行、银行发行和其他银行产品。
note_en:
Mollie, a startup that offers a simple, API-based way to integrate payments into a site or an app, has raised €90 million ($106 million) in a round of funding led by TCV. The Series B brings the total raised by Mollie to €115 million and notably catapults the startup’s valuation to over $1 billion, founder and CEO Adriaan Mol confirmed in an interview with TechCrunch.
 Mollie has been around since 2004 and this is only the second time it’s raised funding — the first was a €25 million round a year ago — which is possibly one reason why it has not been much on the startup radar.
  “It built the backend and front end by myself when I still lived with my parents,” Mol said. “It’s the Dutch way. Bootstrap your idea for a pretty long time. I think that’s the foundation of the company.”
 Yet Mollie has hit a number of milestones over the last several years that provide some explanation for why it has now arrived on the scene, seemingly out of nowhere, with such a high valuation.
 Currently the company focuses mainly on small and medium businesses — a vastly underserved but giant market — and counts some 100,000 merchants as customers, predominantly in the Netherlands, Belgium and Germany. Those customers include some very high-profile names like Wickey, Deliveroo, TOMS (the shoe company) and UNICEF.
 It’s currently on track to process more than €10 billion in transactions this year, representing growth of 100% on a year ago, with some markets like Germany growing 1,000%. And it has been profitable for a number of years at this point.
 “We are on the right side of efficiency,” Mol said — Mollie is his nickname among friends. “But we need to invest in new products to stay competitive.”
 Indeed, the market for payment service providers is a pretty crowded one, with companies like Stripe and Mollie’s compatriot Adyen also building strong businesses on the concept of providing APIs, and a few simple lines of code, to integrate payment flows into other services. These companies are not only popular, but very well capitalised and poised to continue to develop more tools, as well as simply continuing to grow their international footprint.
 Mol says Mollie stands apart from them and the rest of the competition in two key ways. The first is that it provides very localised payment offerings in what remains a very fragmented market, where each consumers’ and merchants’ preferences for what payment methods to use vary a lot country by country (and still are not fully being served by the likes of Stripe).
 The second is that it provides a very quick and simple integration that hides a lot of the difficulty of integrating so many different kinds of payment methods. That ease of use both for merchants and customers mean that there is less shopping cart abandonment, and thus higher conversion rates for site visitors. Mol said that the conversion bump can be typically as high as 7%.
 “If the journey is super slick and smooth, they don’t drop out, and that is direct revenues for our customers,” he said. ( Pricing is transparent but not uniform: it depends on volume, and which payment methods are integrated and used.)
 That — alongside the growth rate of a very profitable and efficient company — was part of what attracted TCV.
 “The ease of use point is really critical,” said John Doran, a partner at TCV. “It’s so easy that a kid could use it. The idea is to build the Apple of the payments world.”
 Mol first came upon the idea of building Mollie when he was integrating a payments service into MessageBird, his previous startup, which provides  API-based messaging services (think of it as Europe’s answer to Twilio). At the time, he said he found all of the payments offerings available to be sub-par and too hard to use in the way that he wanted, and so they — specifically Mol himself, who is an engineer — built the solution in-house (literally, his parents’ house: see above).
 I asked why the company didn’t simply expand and/or pivot to provide both services under one umbrella, but Mol said the two businesses and ideas were simply too big and not similar enough to develop as one business. So he left MessageBird’s executive leadership — he’s still on the board, though — to focus full-time on Mollie.
  The next steps for the company, Mol said, will be to continue building out services adjacent to payments — areas like potentially providing working capital for its customers, along with other financial services that SMBs do not generally get from single providers. “We see a whole bunch of products that traditional banks are not offering to SMEs,” Mol said. “They are not incentivised to invest in them because they do not make much money off them.” Those, he added, could include point of sale payments, card issuing, bank issuing, and other banking products.
title: 荷兰支付初创公司Mollie以超过10亿美元的估值筹集1.06亿美元
title_en: Dutch payments startup Mollie raises $106M at $1B+ valuation

艺电的UFC4开局并不顺利。开发商告诉EuroGamer，在许多粉丝抱怨这一经历后，他们已经从MMA游戏的回放和覆盖中删除了商业广告。这些都不是小的、非侵入性的宣传--一个经常被引用的例子是亚马逊“男孩”第二季的全屏视频广告。UFC 3的玩家也报告了类似的广告。
该公司表示，它在9月初打开了广告，但从反弹来看，“非常清楚”，回放和覆盖中的广告“不受欢迎”。EA表示，这些广告“未来不会再出现”。它补充说，广告对UFC系列来说并不新鲜，但通常是为主菜单标题或八角形标志放置预留的。
批评人士抱怨说，他们不仅在付费游戏中看到了广告，而且时机也不诚实。这些广告出现在UFC 4发布大约两周后，或者在最初的评论之后很久。如果你是一个早期采用者，你不会意识到你正准备进行一场营销闪电战。虽然广告商显然渴望接触到可能看不太多电视的游戏玩家，但EA似乎高估了玩家坐着看任何扰乱游戏的广告的愿望。
这并不是艺电第一次为了应对商业化的喧嚣而不得不改变游戏。最值得注意的是，在玩家对付费获胜的奖品盒机械师犹豫不决后，它改进了星球大战前线II的进阶系统。EA不止一次被指责将售后收入的渴望置于游戏体验之上，而UFC 4与广告的短暂玩弄对其声誉无济于事。
note_en:
EA’s   UFC 4 is off to a rough start. The developer told  Eurogamer it had  removed commercials from the MMA game’s replays and overlays after many fans  complained about the experience. These weren’t small, unintrusive promos — one commonly-cited example was a full-screen video ad for Amazon’s second season of   The Boys.  UFC 3 players have  reported similar ads.
 The company said it turned the ads on in early September, but that it was “abundantly clear” from the backlash that ads in replays and overlays were “not welcome.” These commercials “will not be reappearing in the future,” EA said. It added that ads weren’t new to the  UFC series, but were typically reserved for main menu titles or Octagon logo placements.
  Critics complained not just that they were seeing ads in a paid game, but that the timing was dishonest. The ads appeared roughly two weeks after  UFC 4’s launch, or well after initial reviews. If you were an early adopter, you wouldn’t have realized you were in store for a marketing blitz. While advertisers are clearly eager to reach gamers who might not watch much TV, EA appears to have overestimated players’ desire to sit through any ads that disrupt gameplay.
 This isn’t the first time EA has had to change a game in response to an uproar over commercialization. Most notably, it  revamped Star Wars Battlefront II’s progression system after gamers balked at a pay-to-win loot box mechanic. EA has been accused more than once of putting its desire for post-sale revenue ahead of the gameplay experience, and  UFC 4’s brief dalliance with ads won’t help its reputation.
title: EA在收到广泛投诉后从《UFC 4》重播中撤下广告
title_en: EA Pulls Ads from 'UFC 4' Replays After Widespread Complaints

人们普遍认为，用户名为@jsmith12345678的Twitter账户一定是机器人，或者是巨魔，或者其他邪恶的演员。
问题是，至少早在2017年12月，Twitter注册过程就不允许你选择自己的用户名！取而代之的是，它根据你的名字和姓氏，在末尾加上八个数字，给你一个名字。在此之后，系统不会提示您选择一个更有特色的用户名，您可以更改它，但您需要自己弄清楚如何更改。(2017年12月的日期是一个在Twitter Design工作的人私下向我确认的。)。
这意味着，当你看到某人回复的用户名中有一串数字时，实际上很有可能这个用户只是在2017年12月之后加入Twitter的某个人，要么不介意更改用户名，要么不知道自己可以更改，或者不知道如何更改。换句话说，它可能是一个不太懂技术的用户。
我的观点是：如果一个不是Twitter用户、也不习惯使用Twitter平台的人加入Twitter是为了表达他们的观点，那么他们也很可能有这样的用户名。这并不意味着他们在不守信用地运营：这只是意味着他们对该平台还不熟悉。将用户名采用这种格式的人的观点斥为机器人或巨魔，最终是一种非常粗鲁的方式，意思是说，你是一个新手，你不明白如何在Twitter上表现得像人一样，滚开。就我个人而言，我认为我们应该做得更好。
对于好奇的人，我已经截图了截至2020年8月31日的整个移动Twitter注册过程。我们从一个标准的首页开始。
然后我们被要求输入我们的名字(这里暗示的是真实姓名)，然后是电子邮件/电话，然后是出生日期。我们收到一个验证码，然后被要求输入密码，然后是头像。
这就是向考虑因素迈出的每一步。在注册过程中，他们从来不会要求您选择用户名，而您甚至不知道您的用户名是什么，直到您点击您的个人资料图片：
如果我想要更改它，我必须单击三个级别的设置→帐户→用户名，在那里我会得到更多的建议，其中有很多数字。我也可以在这里输入我自己的用户名。
这就是你作为第一次用户更改用户名的方式。我猜想，大多数不是社交媒体狂热用户、技术水平也不高的人甚至不会想要更改他们的用户名。他们为什么要这么做呢？Twitter运行得很好，可以在对话中显示他们选择的名字，而不会触及用户名设置。
note_en:
There's a common belief that Twitter accounts with usernames like  @jsmith12345678 must be bots, or trolls, or otherwise nefarious actors.
 The thing is, since at least as far back as December 2017, the Twitter signup process has not allowed you to choose your own username! It instead gives you a name based on your first and last name, plus eight numbers on the end. You aren't prompted to pick a more distinctive username after that, and you can change it but you need to figure out how to do it yourself. (The December 2017 date was confirmed to me privately by someone who works at Twitter Design.)
 This means that when you see a reply from someone with a username with a bunch of numbers in it, it's actually pretty likely that the user is simply someone who joined Twitter after December 2017 and either doesn't care to change their username, or doesn't know that they can change it, or doesn't know how to change it. In other words, it's probably a user who isn't very technically savvy.
 Here's my claim: if someone who is not "of Twitter" and is not used to the platform joins Twitter in order to express their opinions, they are also likely to have a username like this. That doesn't mean they are operating in bad faith: it simply means they are new to the platform. Dismissing the opinions of people with usernames in this format as bots or trolls is ultimately a very rude way of saying "you're a newbie, you don't understand how to appear human to me here on Twitter, go away". Personally, I think we should be better than that.
  For the curious, I've screenshotted the entire mobile Twitter signup process as of August 31, 2020. We begin on a standard splash page.
  We are then asked for our name (real name is implied here), and then email/phone, and then date of birth. We get sent a verification code, then are asked for a password, then a profile picture.
    That's every step towards taking an account. They never once ask you to pick a username during signup, and you don't even know what your username is until you click on your profile image:
   If I want to change it, I have to click three levels deep into Settings → Account → Username, where I am prompted with more suggestions that have lots of numbers in them. I can also type my own username in here.
  And that's how you change your username as a first-time user. I suspect that most people who aren't avid users of social media and aren't super technical don't even think to change their username. Why would they? Twitter works perfectly well, and shows their chosen name in conversations, without ever touching the username setting.
title: 在包含大量数字的Twitter用户名上
title_en: On Twitter Usernames With Lots of Numbers

(路透社)-华尔街日报周一报道，中国正在发起一项制定全球数据安全标准的倡议，以对抗美国说服各国将其网络与中国技术隔离的努力。
“华尔街日报”援引其审阅的一份草案称，根据其“全球数据安全倡议”，中国将呼吁所有国家以“全面、客观和循证的方式”处理数据安全问题。
该倡议将敦促各国反对“针对其他国家的大规模监控”，并呼吁科技公司不要“在其产品和服务中安装后门，非法获取用户数据，控制或操纵用户的系统和设备”。
报道称，中国外交部长王毅定于周二在北京举行的全球数字治理研讨会上宣布这一倡议。
报道称，中国外交官已与多个外国政府接洽，寻求他们对北京新倡议的支持。
近几个月来，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)政府以国家安全担忧为由，收紧了对中国公司的限制。华盛顿还推出了一项“清洁网络”倡议，将被视为威胁国家安全的中国科技公司排除在外。
另外，特朗普周一提出了将美国和中国经济分开的想法，暗示如果世界上最大的两个经济体不再做生意，美国将不会亏损。
note_en:
(Reuters) - China is launching an initiative to set global standards on data security, countering U.S. efforts to persuade countries to ringfence their networks from Chinese technology, the Wall Street Journal reported on Monday.
 Under its “Global Initiative on Data Security,” China would call on all countries to handle data security in a “comprehensive, objective and evidence-based manner,” the Journal said, citing a draft that it had reviewed.
 The initiative would urge countries to oppose “mass surveillance against other states” and call on tech companies not to install “backdoors in their products and services to illegally obtain users’ data, control or manipulate users’ systems and devices.”
 Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi is scheduled to announce the initiative on Tuesday at a seminar in Beijing on global digital governance, the report said.
 Chinese diplomats have approached a number of foreign governments to seek their support for Beijing’s new initiative, according to the report.
 The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump in recent months has tightened its restrictions on Chinese companies, citing national security concerns. Washington also rolled out a “Clean Network” initiative to exclude Chinese tech firms perceived as threatening national security.
 Separately, Trump on Monday raised the idea of separating the U.S. and Chinese economies, suggesting the United States would not lose money if the world’s two biggest economies no longer did business.
title: 中国将启动制定全球数据安全规则的倡议
title_en: China To Launch Initiative To Set Global Data-Security Rules

这些功能可能是尖端的，甚至是很酷的，但我们认为买家应该警惕支付8000美元购买电动汽车公司特斯拉(Tesla)所称的全自动驾驶功能选项。特斯拉声称，它制造的每辆新车都包括完全自动驾驶所需的所有硬件，该公司表示，通过未来的空中软件更新，其汽车最终应该能够自动驾驶。
但就目前而言，全面的自动驾驶功能，包括可以帮助司机停车、在高速公路上变道，甚至在红绿灯和停车标志前完全停车的功能，仍然是一个错误的措辞。
近十年来，特斯拉一直将自己与其他汽车制造商区分开来，制造具有行业领先行驶里程的时尚电动汽车，并开发新技术，目标是有朝一日创造一款完全自动驾驶的汽车。尽管特斯拉在自动驾驶方面取得了重大进展，但基于消费者报告的广泛测试和经验，车主不应依赖特斯拉的驾驶员辅助功能来必然地增加安全性或让驾驶变得更容易。
特斯拉完整的自动驾驶功能套件中的大多数功能工作起来都不一致，包括已经存在了几年的Autopark自动停车系统。有时它会认出一个合适的停车位，我们就把车停在里面。但是后来当我们再次经过同一个车位时，就好像那个停车位并不存在一样。它通常也不会直接停在停车线之间。
Smart Conmon允许汽车远程驾驶到私人停车场内的某个位置，有时会在停车场行驶车道的错误一侧行驶，而且它并不总是在停车场的停车标志前停车。
激活自动驾驶导航后，只要目的地已被编程到导航系统中，特斯拉就可以在高速公路上自动上下坡并进行车道更改。我们发现性能不一致，系统有时会忽略设定路线上的出口匝道，在拼车车道上行驶，并长时间停留在通过车道上。该功能有时也会在没有明显原因的情况下完全脱离。
交通灯和停车标志控制设计为在所有红绿灯时完全停止，即使它们是绿色的，除非司机超越系统。我们发现这个系统有几个问题，包括一个基本的想法，那就是汽车在绿灯时开始减速停下来是违反正常驾驶习惯的。有时，它还会驶过停车标志，即使在合并明确的情况下，也会为让行标志猛踩刹车，在绕过一个交通圈时，在每个出口都会停车。
特斯拉的主动驾驶辅助系统分为两部分：第一部分是自动驾驶-包括自适应巡航控制(特斯拉将其称为交通感知巡航控制)和车道保持辅助功能(特斯拉术语中的Autosteer)-现在是每一款新特斯拉的标准功能。
在本次评估中，我们将重点放在驾驶辅助系统的可选全自动驾驶功能套件上：自动标记、自动变道、召唤、智能召唤、自动驾驶导航以及红绿灯和停车标志控制。
下面，我们将解释该套件中的每个功能、其预期用途，以及每个功能在我们的测试中的执行情况。
我们在一天中的不同时间、不同的天气条件和许多不同的地点对完整的自动驾驶功能进行了测试。我们还确保有最新的硬件和软件可用，在我们的测试期间是在硬件3上运行的2020.24.6.4版。这一点很重要，因为特斯拉的空中软件更新可能会改变这些功能的执行方式。
我们为这个故事评估的前三个功能-自动标记、自动车道更改和召唤-已经存在了几年，其中一些已经随着时间的推移而有所改进。然而，特斯拉的最新功能-智能召唤、自动驾驶导航以及红绿灯和停车标志控制-让我们对其实用性和安全性产生了一些重大担忧。
根据特斯拉网站上的信息，Autopark“只需一次触摸，就可以帮助你的汽车自动平行或垂直停放”。
该系统的工作原理是这样的：当你巡航经过停车场的常规或平行停车点时，中央显示屏上会弹出一个“P”，表示系统已经找到了合适的停车点。轻触触摸屏上的“P”键，将档位选择器换成倒档，系统将自动处理停车涉及的一切，包括转向和刹车。
我们发现它经常像广告中说的那样起作用，但其他时候却很难吸引人。在这些情况下，当你开车经过它们时，它不会识别出看起来合适的地方。这是一个问题，因为您不能“强迫”系统工作-它必须自己识别合适的位置。似乎没有一致的理由让它有时在我们开车经过时认出一个停车位，然后又没有注意到另一个驾车经过的完全相同的地方。类似的功能多年来一直存在于其他汽车制造商的汽车上，它们可能会遇到同样的问题。
CR联网和自动化车辆测试负责人凯利·芬克哈勒(Kelly Funkhoer)表示：“Autopark也经常不会在字里行间直接停车，以至于如果我们下车后意识到自己停得如此歪斜，我们会感到尴尬。”
虽然这不是特斯拉汽车独有的，但这一功能很简单，效果正如广告所说的那样。根据特斯拉的说法，它“在Autosteer投入使用时，可以帮助移动到高速公路上的相邻车道”。
如果司机在自动驾驶激活时点击转向灯手柄换道，汽车将进行换道操作，而不需要司机的任何转向输入。屏幕上会出现一个动画，显示汽车将移动到的位置，如果您要移动到的车道上有一辆汽车，它将等待进行车道转换。
“不过，最终每次换道的安全仍然是司机的责任，”费舍尔说。
此功能旨在允许汽车在没有驾驶员在方向盘后面的情况下向前或向后行驶一小段距离。根据特斯拉网站的说法，召唤“使用移动应用程序或钥匙将你的车移入和移出一个狭窄的空间”。
当在智能手机上使用特斯拉应用程序时，你可以通过按下手机或钥匙卡上的相应按钮来引导汽车缓慢前进或后退到某个位置，甚至可以对它进行编程，以打开和关闭你的车库门。我们发现它确实执行了这些功能，我们做了一些测试来模拟把车挤进一个相当狭窄的停车点，在稍微拥挤的停车线上的汽车之间，它能够完成这个动作。
不过，它的工作方式并不一致。我们的测试人员发现，有时系统会在停车位内排得很好，但随后只会回到现场的一半位置，留下部分汽车伸出。其他时候，它会以一定的角度停在停车位上，然后就到此为止。
合乎逻辑的是，认为召唤最实际的用途是帮助解决停车位紧张的问题，这样你就不需要把自己挤进车里或挤出车外。然而，CR的特斯拉Model 3的车主手册上写道，“在狭窄的空间内停车限制了传感器准确探测障碍物位置的能力，增加了Model 3和/或周围物体受损的风险。”
芬克哈勒说：“不幸的是，特斯拉并没有给出召唤的最佳使用案例，这让我们感到困惑，如果它不是为了在狭小的空间停车，那么这个系统的真正目的是什么--除了让你的朋友们看到你的车可以远程驾驶之外。”“在这些演习中，你还需要密切关注赛车，以确保它不会撞上任何东西。”
顾名思义，智能召唤是一个比普通召唤更高级的功能。特斯拉表示，它是“为了让你的车开到你身边或你选择的地点而设计的。”特斯拉的网站进一步表示，有了Smart Summon，“你的车将在更复杂的环境和停车位中导航，根据需要绕过物体，在停车场找到你。”
使用特斯拉智能手机应用程序，司机可以在地图上精确定位汽车的目的地，或者使用来我按钮让汽车开到他们那里。
我们在许多场景中试用了智能召唤，包括纽约州扬克斯市CR总部的停车场。总体而言，该系统被证明是不可靠的。有时它会走非常复杂的路线才能到达我们，而在其他情况下，它可能会卡在斜坡上，然后完全停用。
即使智能召唤能够到达指定的位置，它也会在停车场的错误车道上花费时间，不会因为停车场停车标志而停下来，或者大转弯向停放的汽车驶去，然后倒车以避免碰撞，如上面的动画所示。这些情况都可能会让其他司机感到困惑，也会对汽车路径上的任何行人构成潜在的危险。
特斯拉在其网站上明确表示，“智能召唤仅适用于私人停车场和车道。你仍然对你的车负责， 必须时刻监控它和它的周围环境， 必须在你的视线之内，因为它可能无法检测到所有障碍物。“。私人停车场可以包括商店或餐馆的停车场，这意味着你可以把智能召唤视为你自己的个人贴身服务。
我们可以看到这项功能的好处，例如，在下雨天，让您的车在商店入口处附近迎接您和您的食品杂货。但我们的测试显示，特斯拉还没有解决所有的错误。“有时它看起来不稳定，”芬克哈勒说。“我们认为，在客户体验到系统的不一致行为后，他们很可能不会再使用它。”
这一功能扩展了现有的自动驾驶系统，增加了特斯拉在高速公路上行驶的能力，当目的地被编程到汽车的导航系统中时，无需司机输入即可进行车道更改。根据特斯拉的说法，它“主动引导你的汽车从高速公路的入口匝道到出口匝道，包括建议换道，导航立交桥，自动进入转向灯，并选择正确的出口”。
虽然该系统实际上可以做所有这些事情，但它远远不是一致的。在一种情况下，系统完全忽略了它应该走的出口；这不仅使我们的测试员偏离了他们的路线，而且还将汽车卡在了只有一个人在车内的拼车车道上。
在另一个例子中，系统突然脱离，在一个交通繁忙的地区，随着一个入口匝道的快速接近，给我们的司机造成了混乱。然而，正是在这种情况下，司机需要专注于他们周围的环境，而不是因为驾驶功能突然出现问题而分心。
我们还发现，Autopilot上的导航偶尔会让我们在高速公路上的过路车道上行驶很长一段时间，而在这种情况下，最安全的做法-更不用说适当的车道礼仪和一些州法律-需要回到中心车道或右车道。
我们还担心，自动驾驶导航可以允许司机将该功能设置为自动进行车道更改，而不需要司机激活转向灯手柄，也不需要系统向司机发出即将发生车道更改的警告。
“允许司机关闭换道确认和警告的设置令人担忧，如果不是彻头彻尾的危险的话，”费舍尔说。“这可能会导致司机在高速行驶时被意外且不安全的变道吓了一跳。”
这是加入全自动驾驶功能包的最新功能，它与自适应巡航控制和车道保持辅助一起使用。特斯拉说，当Autopilot启动时，这个系统“在你的积极监督下，识别停车标志和红绿灯，并在你的积极监督下，在接近时自动减速停车。”如果十字路口是空的，司机可以轻踩油门踏板或拉下选档杆来保持汽车行驶。
根据特斯拉自己的设计，这一功能将使汽车在任何红绿灯下完全停车，无论红灯是红灯、黄灯还是绿灯。特斯拉唯一会通过绿灯而不会自动停车的情况是，如果前面有一辆领先的车辆，它可以通过红灯跟随-或者如果你超越了系统。
除了汽车在绿灯时开始减速停车违反正常驾驶礼仪外，我们还发现，我们的特斯拉偶尔会在距离停车标志太远的地方踩刹车，导致我们的司机不得不多次不停地踩油门踏板或选档杆才能把车一直开到停车标志。在其他情况下，汽车根本无法识别停车标志，迫使我们的测试员不得不踩刹车，以避免直接通过停车标志进入十字路口，如上面的动画所示。
该系统在环行交叉路口时也存在问题，在绕过交通环路时在每个出口处停车。其他时候，即使合并是明确的，它也会在接近屈服迹象时猛踩刹车。
“在目前的迭代中，我们认为特斯拉的红绿灯和停车标志控制将给司机带来更多的困惑和分心，”费舍尔说。
特斯拉在中央显示屏上将自动驾驶导航和红绿灯和停车标志控制功能标记为“Beta”，这通常意味着计算机软件仍在开发中。虽然你确实必须选择使用这些功能，但我们的测试显示，在日常驾驶中使用它们存在一些问题。
在这篇文章中，我们在一封电子邮件中询问了特斯拉对Beta的定义，我们问司机如何区分列出为Beta的功能与不是Beta的功能。特斯拉没有回应。
消费者报告安全政策经理威廉·华莱士(William Wallace)表示：“特斯拉一再推出简陋的测试版功能，其中一些功能可能会危及人们的安全，除了在私人测试赛道或试验场之外，不应该在任何地方使用。”
特斯拉表示，这些功能将随着时间的推移而改进，但在这一点上，它们似乎不值得8000美元的高价。
“似乎特斯拉专注于成为拥有最多功能的汽车制造商，而不是确保这些功能运行良好，”芬克豪勒说。它的时间和精力可以更好地用于开发自动驾驶的驾驶员监控系统，以显著提高该系统的安全性和实用性。“。
特斯拉会从那些购买其汽车的人那里收集数据，以帮助改进它，但在道路上使用这样的开发技术对你和其他司机来说并不是没有风险。我们将继续评估这些和其他主动驾驶辅助系统，因为它们是可用的。
note_en:
The features might be cutting edge, even cool, but we think buyers should be wary of shelling out $8,000 for what electric car company  Tesla calls its  Full Self-Driving Capability option. Tesla claims every new vehicle it builds includes all the hardware necessary to be fully autonomous, and the company says that through future over-the-air software updates, its cars should eventually be capable of driving themselves.
 But for now, Full Self-Driving Capability, which includes features that can assist the driver with parking,  changing lanes on the highway, and even coming to a complete halt at traffic lights and stop signs, remains a misnomer.
  Tesla has distinguished itself from other carmakers for almost a decade by building sleek electric cars with industry-leading driving ranges and by developing new technology aimed at one day creating a fully autonomous, self-driving car. Though it has made significant strides in automated driving, owners should not rely on Tesla’s driver assistance features to necessarily add safety or to make driving easier, based on Consumer Reports’ extensive testing and experience.
  Most features within Tesla’s Full Self-Driving Capability suite worked inconsistently, including the  Autopark self-parking system that has been around for several years. Sometimes it would recognize a parking space as suitable, and we’d park in it. But when we drove by the same space again later, it was as if the parking spot didn’t exist. It also often didn’t park straight between the parking lines.
 Smart Summon,  which allows the car to drive remotely to a location within a private parking lot, would sometimes drive on the wrong side of parking lot driving lanes, and it didn’t always stop at stop signs in the lot.
 Navigate on Autopilot, when activated, allows a Tesla traveling on the highway to autonomously take on- and off-ramps and make lane changes as long as a destination has been programmed into the navigation system. We found the performance to be inconsistent, with the system sometimes ignoring exit ramps on the set route, driving in the carpool lane, and staying in the passing lane for long periods of time. The feature also would completely disengage at times for no apparent reason.
 Traffic Light and Stop Sign Control is designed to come to a complete stop at all stoplights, even when they are green, unless the driver overrides the system. We found several problems with this system, including the basic idea that it goes against normal driving practice for a car to start slowing to a stop for a green light. At times, it also drove through stop signs, slammed on the brakes for yield signs even when the merge was clear, and stopped at every exit while going around a traffic circle.
 Tesla’s active driving assistance systems are split into two parts: The first, Autopilot—which includes adaptive cruise control (Tesla refers to this as Traffic-Aware Cruise Control) and lane keeping assistance capabilities (Autosteer in Tesla terminology)—is now standard on every new Tesla.
 For this evaluation, we focused on the optional Full Self-Driving Capability suite of driving assistance systems: Autopark, Auto Lane Change, Summon, Smart Summon, Navigate on Autopilot, and Traffic Light and Stop Sign Control.
 Below, we explain each feature in the suite, its intended use, and how each performed in our tests.
  We ran our tests of the Full Self-Driving Capability features at various times of the day, in a variety of weather conditions, and in many different locations. We also made sure we had the most up-to-date hardware and software available, which during our testing was version 2020.24.6.4 running on Hardware 3. This is important to note, because Tesla’s over-the-air software updates can alter the way these features perform.
 The first three features we evaluated for this story—Autopark, Auto Lane Change, and Summon—have been around for a few years, and some have improved over that time. However, Tesla’s most recent features—Smart Summon, Navigate on Autopilot, and Traffic Light and Stop Sign Control—gave us some significant concerns about their usefulness and safety.
 Autopark “helps automatically parallel or perpendicular park your car, with a single touch,” according to information on Tesla’s website.
 Here’s how the system works: As you cruise by either a regular or parallel parking spot in a parking lot, a “P” will pop up on the center display screen indicating that the system has found an appropriate spot. Tap the “P” on the touch screen and shift the gear selector into Reverse, and the system will automatically handle everything involved with parking the car, including steering and braking.
 We found that it often worked as advertised, but other times it had difficulty engaging. In those cases, it wouldn’t recognize spots that seemed appropriate as you drove past them. This is a problem because you can’t “force” the system to work—it has to recognize an appropriate spot on its own. There seemed to be no consistent reason for it to sometimes recognize a parking spot as we drove by it and then not notice the exact same spot on another drive-by. Similar features have been around for years on cars from other automakers, and they can suffer from the same problems.
 “Autopark also often didn’t park straight between the lines, to the point that we’d be embarrassed of ourselves if we got out of the car and realized we parked in such a cockeyed fashion,” says Kelly Funkhouser, head of connected and automated vehicle testing at CR.
 While not unique to Tesla vehicles, this feature is straightforward and works as advertised. It “assists in moving to an adjacent lane on the highway when Autosteer is engaged,” according to Tesla.
 If the driver hits the turn-signal stalk to change lanes when Autopilot is activated, the car will perform the lane-change maneuver without any steering input needed from the driver. An animation appears on screen that shows where the car will move to, and it will wait to make the lane change if there is a car in the lane you want to move into.
 “Ultimately, though, the safety of each lane change is still the driver’s responsibility,” Fisher says.
 This feature is designed to allow the car to drive a short distance forward or backward without a driver behind the wheel. Summon “moves your car in and out of a tight space using the mobile app or key,” according to Tesla’s website.
 When using the Tesla app on a smartphone, you can direct the car slowly forward or backward into a spot by pressing the corresponding button on the phone or key fob, and it can even be programmed to open and close your garage door. We found that it did, in fact, perform these functions, and we did some tests to mimic squeezing the car into a fairly tight parking spot between cars that were crowding the parking lane lines slightly, and it was able to complete the maneuver.
 It works inconsistently, though. Our testers found that sometimes the system would line up well within the parking space but then back only halfway into the spot, leaving part of the car sticking out. Other times it would pull into the parking spot at an angle and call it a day.
 It’s logical to think that Summon’s most practical use is to help deal with tight parking spaces so that you won’t have to squeeze yourself into or out of the car. However, the owner’s manual of CR’s  Tesla Model 3 says that “parking in a narrow space limits the ability of the sensors to accurately detect the location of obstacles, increasing the risk of damage to Model 3 and/or surrounding objects.”
 “Unfortunately, Tesla doesn’t give a best-use case for Summon, which leaves us perplexed as to what the system is really intended for if it’s not for parking in tight spaces—other than to impress your friends that your car can drive remotely,” Funkhouser says. “You also need to keep a very close eye on the car during these maneuvers to make sure it doesn’t run into anything.”
  As the name implies,  Smart Summon is intended to be a considerably more advanced feature than regular Summon. Tesla says it is “designed to allow your car to drive to you or a location of your choosing.” Tesla’s website further states that with Smart Summon, “your car will navigate more complex environments and parking spaces, maneuvering around objects as necessary to come find you in a parking lot.”
 Using the Tesla smartphone app, the driver can either pinpoint a destination spot on the map for the car, or have the car drive to them using the Come to Me button.
 We tried out Smart Summon in a number of scenarios, including the parking lot at CR headquarters in Yonkers, N.Y. Overall, the system proved to be unreliable. At times it would take overly complicated routes to reach us, and in other situations it might get stuck on an incline, then deactivate completely.
 Even when Smart Summon was able to arrive at the specified location, it would spend time in the wrong lane of the parking lot, not pause for parking-lot stop signs, or take wide turns and head toward parked cars and then drive in Reverse to avoid a collision, as shown in the animation above. These are all situations that could cause confusion to other drivers, as well as be a potential hazard for any pedestrians in the car’s path.
 Tesla states clearly on its website that “Smart Summon is only intended for use in private parking lots and driveways. You are still responsible for your car and must monitor it and its surroundings at all times and be within your line of sight because it may not detect all obstacles.” Private parking lots can include those for stores or restaurants, which means you can think of Smart Summon as your own personal valet service.
 We can see the benefits of this feature, for instance having your car drive to meet you and your groceries near the store entrance on a rainy day. But Tesla doesn’t have all the bugs worked out yet, our testing shows. “At times it appeared erratic,” Funkhouser says. “We think after a customer experiences the system’s inconsistent behavior, they will likely not use it again.”
 This feature expands the existing Autopilot system, adding the ability for a Tesla that’s traveling on the highway to take on- and off-ramps, and make lane changes without driver input when a destination has been programmed into the car’s navigation system. It “actively guides your car from a highway’s on-ramp to off-ramp, including suggesting lane changes, navigating interchanges, automatically engaging the turn signal and taking the correct exit,” according to Tesla.
 While the system can, in fact, do all of those things, it was far from consistent. In one case, the system completely ignored the exit it was supposed to take; this not only put our tester off of their route but also stuck the car in a carpool lane with only one person in the vehicle.
 In another example, the system suddenly disengaged, causing confusion for our driver in a heavily trafficked area with an on-ramp quickly approaching. Yet it’s in situations exactly like this that a driver needs to be focused on the environment around them, not distracted with a driving feature suddenly having an problem.
 We also found that  Navigate on Autopilot would occasionally leave us driving in the passing lane on a highway for long periods of time, when the safest course of action—not to mention proper lane etiquette and some state laws—requires moving back over to the center or right lane in that situation.
 We are also concerned that Navigate on Autopilot can allow a driver to set the feature to automatically make  lane changes on its own, without the driver activating the turn-signal stalk and without the system giving a warning to the driver that a lane change is about to happen.
 “The setting that allows the driver to turn off the system’s lane-change confirmations and warnings is worrying, if not downright dangerous,” Fisher says. “It could lead to drivers becoming startled by an unexpected and unsafe lane change while driving at highway speeds.”
  This is the most recent feature to join the Full Self-Driving Capability package, and it is used in conjunction with adaptive cruise control and lane keeping assistance. Tesla says when Autopilot is engaged, this system “identifies stop signs and traffic lights and automatically slows your car to a stop on approach, with your active supervision.” If an intersection is clear, the driver can tap the accelerator pedal or pull down on the gear selector stalk to keep the car going.
 By Tesla’s own design, this feature will bring the car to a complete stop at any traffic light, regardless of whether the light is red, yellow, or green. The only time the Tesla will drive through a green light without stopping on its own is if there is a lead vehicle in front that it can follow through the light—or if you override the system.
 In addition to the fact that it goes against normal driving etiquette for a car to start slowing to a stop for a green light, we also found that our Tesla would occasionally hit the brakes too far away from a stop sign, causing our driver to have to keep hitting the accelerator pedal or the gear selector stalk multiple times to move the car all the way to the stop sign. In other circumstances, the car simply wouldn’t recognize the stop sign at all, forcing our tester to have to hit the brakes to avoid driving right through the stop sign and into the intersection, as shown in the animation above.
 The system also had problems navigating a roundabout, stopping at each exit as it went around the traffic circle. Other times it would slam on the brakes when approaching a yield sign, even if the merge was clear.
 “In its current iteration, we think Tesla’s Traffic Light and Stop Sign Control will create more confusion and distraction for the driver,” Fisher says.
 Tesla labels the Navigate on Autopilot and Traffic Light and Stop Sign Control features as “Beta” on the center display screen, which usually means the computer software is still in development. While you do have to opt in to use these features, our testing demonstrated a number of problems with using them in everyday driving.
 For this article, we asked Tesla in an email for its definition of Beta and we asked how a driver can tell what separates features listed as Beta from those that are not. Tesla did not respond.
 “Tesla has repeatedly rolled out crude beta features, some of which can put people’s safety at risk and shouldn’t be used anywhere but on a private test track or proving ground,” says William Wallace, manager of safety policy for Consumer Reports.
 Tesla says these features will improve over time, but at this point they don’t seem worth the hefty $8,000 price tag.
 “It seems like Tesla is focused on being the automaker with the most features rather than ensuring that the features work well,” Funkhouser says. “Its time and energy could be better spent on developing a driver monitoring system for Autopilot to significantly improve the safety and usefulness of that system.”
 Tesla collects data from those who buy its cars to help improve it, but using developing technology like this on the road does not come without risk for you and other drivers. We’ll continue to evaluate these and other active driving assistance systems as they are available.
title: 评论：提供一些高级功能的特斯拉“全自动驾驶能力”选项仍需要司机高度关注，以降低安全风险
title_en: Review: Tesla's “Full Self-Driving Capability” option, which offers some advanced features, still requires significant driver attention to mitigate safety risks

note:
作为Netflix的创始人和联席首席执行官，里德·黑斯廷斯重塑了人们看电视的方式和娱乐业的运作方式。
1997年，黑斯廷斯与马克·兰道夫(Marc Randolph)合作推出了Netflix，这是一项DVD邮寄电影租赁服务，他很早就意识到互联网是发行的未来。先是凭借好莱坞制片厂和电视网非常乐意出售的老电影和电视剧，然后是“纸牌屋”(House Of Cards)和“奇异物语”(Stranger Things)等原创节目，这家流媒体巨头在全球建立了近2亿家庭的全球订户基础。
一路走来，黑斯廷斯建立了一种独特的--对一些人来说，是残酷的--企业文化。Netflix的方式鼓励员工承担很大的风险，通常不需要一系列老板的批准，并以直率的方式进行沟通。领导者经常练习所谓的“保管员测试”，他们会问自己：如果一名员工被提供到其他地方工作，你会努力留住那名员工吗？如果答案是否定的，这个人就会被解雇。
在他的新书“无规则规则：Netflix与再创造的文化”(No Rules Rules：Netflix and the Culture of Reinovative)中，黑斯廷斯将受雇于这家流媒体巨头比作是运动队的一员：被裁员令人失望，但不会带来耻辱。Netflix的座右铭之一写道：“与许多公司不同的是，我们的做法是：足够的业绩可以获得丰厚的遣散费。”
黑斯廷斯在他儿子位于加州圣克鲁斯家中的卧室通过视频接受了“华尔街日报”的采访。以下是经过编辑的节选。
“华尔街日报”：现在有这么多员工在家工作，Netflix文化中的哪些元素更难维持？
黑斯廷斯先生：没有。我看不到任何积极的东西。不能亲自聚在一起，特别是在国际上，这纯粹是一种负面影响。人们的牺牲给我留下了非常深刻的印象。
“华尔街日报”：据预计，即使在新冠肺炎危机之后，许多公司也会让很多员工转向在家工作。你认为如何？
黑斯廷斯：如果非要我猜的话，一周五天的工作会变成四天在办公室，而一天实际上就是在家。我敢打赌，那一定是很多公司的下场。
“华尔街日报”：你有没有想好员工回归办公室的日期？
黑斯廷斯：可能是疫苗接种后六个月。一旦我们能让大多数人接种疫苗，它很可能就会回到办公室。
“华尔街日报”：你在书中说，“不可能知道像我们这样的企业五年后会发展到什么地步。”你是做什么预测的？
黑斯廷斯：我们一直在尝试实验。五年后，商业模式将非常相似。我们能想出动画吗？我们能在家庭动画中赶上迪士尼吗？
“华尔街日报”：您说过希望Netflix能够抓住意想不到的机会。有哪一个例子是你没有预料到的？
黑斯廷斯：非虚构类节目相当不错。我们从超级优质电视开始，扩展到非虚构类节目取得了巨大的成功。在世界各地分享内容取得了巨大的成功。在此之前，人们认为美国人不会观看在美国以外制作的内容。
“华尔街日报”：Netflix讲究的是激进的坦率。从人事管理的角度来看，人们之间到底能有多坦诚呢？
黑斯廷斯：我们希望人们非常有建设性。我们不想让人们像醉酒的傻瓜一样四处走动，对人们说可怕的话。我们希望人们怀着积极的意愿参与进来，创造一个人们通过获得反馈而茁壮成长的环境。
比如当你做俯卧撑或跑步的时候--很疼，但是你知道你正在变得更强壮。你必须从反馈的角度来考虑这一点：它会给学习带来足够的不适，但又不会造成太多的不适，以至于你攻击这个人，或者感觉是这样的。
“华尔街日报”：你写道，“只有不忙的CEO才能真正做好本职工作。”我相信很多CEO和他们的家人都不会这么认为。你能详细解释一下吗？
黑斯廷斯：作为首席执行长，你不会想被这些策略消耗掉的。对我来说，做出选角决定或产品功能决定-有太多的事情要做。你太忙了，所以，即使你擅长这项工作，你也不会考虑企业的长期健康和发展。你想真正知道所有地方都在发生什么，但不想做决定。
“华尔街日报”：您最近选择与首席内容官泰德·萨兰多斯(Ted Sarandos)分享CEO头衔。企业界充斥着联席CEO架构运作不佳的例子。
黑斯廷斯：这里充斥着单身首席执行长也不合群的例子。当然，联合首席执行官是一件不同寻常的事情。只有当两个人真正合作得很好时，它才能很好地发挥作用。泰德和我已经在一起工作了20多年了。他已经担任虚拟联席首席执行官几年了，我们刚刚决定将其公诸于众。
“华尔街日报”：Netflix以支付市场最高工资而闻名。你担心这会推高你和其他好莱坞人的成本吗？
黑斯廷斯：当你看到伟大的运动队时，他们往往是那些可以高价购买最优秀球员的球队。我们希望拥有绝对最好的球员，薪酬是其中的一部分。我们宁愿要三个杰出的人，也不愿要四个还可以的人。
“华尔街日报”：你写道，价值或创意价值不应该用时间来衡量，而且你从来没有注意过人们的工作时间。然而，Netflix的许多人将其描述为一种全天候的生活方式。缺乏工作与生活的平衡或潜在的倦怠让你担心吗？
黑斯廷斯：回到田径运动，想想教练的观点：重要的不是你在健身房花了多少小时，而是你打得有多好。但如果你要打精英级别的比赛，你可能会经常去健身房。这不是目标状态。目标状态就是有效性。
黑斯廷斯：这本书是为试图非常有创造力的较小组织和较新的组织写的。对于像WarnerMedia或迪士尼这样的大公司来说，他们会读到它，然后有点翻白眼，这很好。希望它能帮助那些仍在努力弄清楚自己想要成为什么样子的小公司。
黑斯廷斯：政治是艰难的，因为从很多方面来说，人们都会选那些经常撒谎的人。在商业上，我们真的努力避免这种情况。在政治上取得成功的技能与在商业上取得成功的技能确实有很大的不同。
黑斯廷斯：我们已经在欧洲大部分地区和亚洲大部分地区开始运营，我们在(洛杉矶)已经有一些事情正在进行。我们的希望是，在9月和10月，通过适当的测试，我们可以真正跑得更多。
黑斯廷斯：我们预计明年的原创图书会比今年多。这真是太不可思议了。
note_en:
As a founder and co-chief executive of  Netflix    NFLX  -1.84%  Reed Hastings has reshaped both the way people watch television and how the entertainment industry operates.
  Launching Netflix in 1997 as a DVD-by-mail movie-rental service with Marc Randolph, Mr. Hastings grasped early that the internet was the future of distribution. First with old movies and TV shows that Hollywood studios and networks were more than happy to sell, then with original programming such as “House of Cards” and “Stranger Things,” the streaming giant has built a global subscriber base approaching  200 million households world-wide.
  Along the way, Mr. Hastings has built a distinctive—and, to some, cutthroat—corporate culture. The Netflix way encourages staff to take big risks, typically without the need of approval from a chain of bosses, and to communicate with blunt candor. Leaders often practice what is called  the “keeper test,” in which they ask themselves: If a staffer were offered a job elsewhere, would you fight to keep that employee? If the answer is no, the person is let go.
  In his new book “No Rules Rules: Netflix and the Culture of Reinvention,” Mr. Hastings likens being employed at the streaming giant to being part of a sports team: Getting cut is disappointing but carries no shame. “Unlike many companies, we practice: Adequate performance gets a generous severance package,” reads one of Netflix’s mottos.
    Mr. Hastings spoke with The Wall Street Journal by video from his son’s bedroom in the family home in Santa Cruz, Calif. Here are edited excerpts.
  WSJ: What elements of the Netflix culture are tougher to maintain now that so many employees are working from home?
      Mr. Hastings: No. I don’t see any positives. Not being able to get together in person, particularly internationally, is a pure negative. I’ve been super impressed at people’s sacrifices.
  WSJ: It’s been anticipated that many companies will shift to a work-from-home approach for many employees even after the Covid-19 crisis. What do you think?
  Mr. Hastings: If I had to guess, the five-day workweek will become four days in the office while one day is virtual from home. I’d bet that’s where a lot of companies end up.
  WSJ: Do you have a date in mind for when your workforce returns to the office?
      Mr. Hastings: It’s probably six months after a vaccine. Once we can get a majority of people vaccinated, then it’s probably back in the office.
  WSJ: In the book you say, “It’s impossible to know where a business like ours will be in five years.” What kind of prognosticating do you do?
  Mr. Hastings: We keep trying experiments. The business model will be pretty similar in five years. Can we figure out animation? Can we catch  Disney in family animation?
  WSJ: You’ve said you want Netflix to be able to pounce on unanticipated opportunities. What’s an example of one you didn’t see coming?
  Mr. Hastings: Nonfiction programming is a pretty good one. We started as superpremium TV, and the expansion into nonfiction has been a huge success. The whole sharing of content around the world has been a huge success. Prior to that, people thought Americans won’t watch content that’s produced outside the U.S.
  WSJ: Netflix is about radical candor. Just how candid can people be with each other from a personnel-management standpoint?
  Mr. Hastings: We want people to be very constructive. We don’t want people to go around like a drunken fool, saying terrible things to people. We want people to be engaged with positive intent, to create an environment where people thrive from getting feedback.
    Like when you do push-ups or when you run—it hurts, but you know you’re getting stronger. You have to think about it in terms of giving feedback: It’s producing enough discomfort for learning, but not so much discomfort that you’re attacking the person or it feels like that.
  WSJ: You write, ”Only a CEO who is not busy is really doing their job.” I’m sure a lot of CEOs and their families would beg to differ. Can you elaborate?
  Mr. Hastings: You don’t want to be, as CEO, consumed by the tactics. For me, making casting decisions or product feature decisions—there are too many to make. You get too busy so that, even if you are good at it, you’re not thinking about the long-term health and evolution of the business. You want to really know what’s going on in all kinds of places, but not making decisions.
  WSJ: You recently chose to  share the CEO title with Chief Content Officer Ted Sarandos. The corporate world is littered with examples of co-CEO structures that haven’t worked out well.
  Mr. Hastings: It’s littered with examples of single CEOs not working out also. Co-CEOs are an unusual thing, for sure. It only works well when two people really work well together. Ted and I have been working together for more than 20 years. He’s been a virtual co-CEO for a couple of years, and we just decided to make it official.
  WSJ: Netflix is known for paying top-of-the-market salaries. Are you concerned that has driven up costs for you and others in Hollywood?
  Mr. Hastings: When you’re looking at great sports teams, they’re often the teams that can pay high for the best players. We want to have the absolute best players and compensation is one part of that. We’d rather have three outstanding people than four OK people.
  WSJ: You wrote that value or creative worth shouldn’t be measured by time and that you’ve never paid attention to the hours people are working. Yet many people at Netflix describe it as a 24/7 lifestyle. Does the lack of work-life balance or potential for burnout concern you?
  Mr. Hastings: Coming back to the athletics, think about a coach’s view: It’s not how many hours you spend in the gym, but how well you play. But if you’re going to play at an elite level, you’re probably in the gym quite a bit. It’s just not the goal state. The goal state is the effectiveness.
    Mr. Hastings: The book is for smaller organizations and newer organizations that are trying to be very creative. For big companies like WarnerMedia or Disney, they’ll read it and kind of roll their eyes, and that’s fine. Hopefully it will help smaller companies that are still trying to figure out what they’re trying to be.
    Mr. Hastings: Politics is tough because in many ways people elect people who lie a lot. In business, we really try to avoid that. The skills to succeed in politics are really quite different than in business.
    Mr. Hastings: We’re up and running in much of Europe and much of Asia, and we’ve got a few things going on already in [Los Angeles]. The hope is that, through September and October, we can really get—with proper testing—a lot more running.
    Mr. Hastings: We expect to have more original titles next year than this year. That’s pretty incredible.

养成习惯大约需要两周时间；在新环境中适应大约需要两周时间。一个常见的错误是，把一份新报告的头几周当作大学迎新--社交的、轻松的、了解你的东西。如果您的报告在前两周阅读C#文档，并与团队一起外出吃午饭，猜猜会发生什么，他们只是将这种行为正常化为角色。
答案是：尽快开始你的报告做实实在在的工作。如果他们是软件工程师，他们应该在第一周(理想情况下是第二天或第三天)提交代码；如果他们是产品经理，他们应该在类似的时间框架内参加会议并处理支持活动。那么基调已经定下了-在这里工作意味着把事情做好。
这意味着作为经理，你需要在第一周给你的报告带来轻松的工作。经理们没有准备好开始一份报告，这是人们最终将业绩不佳正常化的首要原因，因为他们没有什么有意义的事情可做。如果一位经理将一份报告描述为在最初几周内没有主动性，这是一个危险信号-经理的工作是为新员工提供明确的立即做出贡献的途径。
人们落入入职陷阱的另一个主要原因是您的组织入职非常缓慢。没完没了的人力资源视频、缓慢的安全流程、堆积如山的脆弱技术设置--所有这些都让一家公司的开局糟糕且适得其反。优化你的入职培训，让员工做你雇他们做的事情。寻找正式的入职绩效指标。例如，对于工程师来说，这可能是第一次提交的时间和加入的时间冲刺。
这一方法也适用于经理和高管。在这群人中，人们在头几周打着“了解正在发生的事情”的幌子四处闲逛，这也是很常见的，也是一种常见的危险信号。经理和高管应该从第一天开始就参与团队的决策，并在最初的几周内开发工件-计划、待办事项列表、战略文档。有了优秀的经理和高管，你会看到他们在第一天就为谈话增添了视角和洞察力，每个人都对聘用到了合适的人表示赞赏。
入职是一个人在公司生活中最关键的时期之一。这是管理员工时投资回报率最高的时期之一。抓住这个机会适当地设定期望，并量化衡量成功。
note_en:
It takes roughly 2 weeks to form a habit; it takes roughly two weeks to get comfortable in a new environment. A common mistake is to treat a new report’s first couple weeks like college orientation - social, light hearted, get-to-know-you stuff. If your report spends the first two weeks reading C# documentation and having lunch out on the town with the team, guess what, they’ve just normalized that behavior as what the role is.
 The answer: start your report doing real work as soon as possible. If they’re a software engineer, they should be committing code week one (ideally day 2 or 3); If they’re a product manager, they should be attending meetings and picking up supporting activities on a similar time frame. Then the tone has been set - working here means getting things done.
 This means as manager you need to give your report tractable work in the first week. Managers not being ready for a report to start is the number one reason people end up normalizing underperformance, because they had nothing meaningful to do. If a manager describes a report as not having initiative in the first couple weeks it’s a red flag - it’s the manager’s job to provide new hires with clear paths to contribute immediately.
 Another main reason people fall into onboarding traps is because your organization has painfully slow onboarding. Endless HR videos, slow security processes, a mountain of fragile technology setup - these all make for a shitty and counterproductive start at a company. Optimize your onboarding to get people doing what you hired them to do. Look to formalize performance indicators of your onboarding. For example, for engineers this could be time-to-first-commit and time-to-joining-sprints.
 This approach holds for managers and executives as well. In that cohort it’s also common and a common red flag for people to spend the first couple weeks putzing around under the guise of “getting to know what’s going on”. Managers and executives should be involved in the decisions of the team from day 1 and developing artifacts - plans, TODO lists, strategy documents - in the first couple weeks. With good managers and executives you see them day 1 adding perspective and insight into conversations, and everyone collectively appreciates that the right person has been hired.
 Onboarding is one of the most critical periods in a person’s time at a company. It’s one of the highest ROI periods when managing someone. Take that opportunity to set expectations properly and measure success quantiatively.
title: 前两周的语气对成功入职至关重要
title_en: The tone of the first two weeks is critical to successful onboarding

对应用程序订阅的传统看法是价格合理，比如1.99美元/月，希望能以较低的价格吸引大量订户。然后，通过低价，更少的人会拒绝订阅的想法，而你将用数量来弥补差额。但这是错误的方法，…。
现实情况是，即使价格很低，大多数人也不会注册你的订阅。大多数人不喜欢订阅，很多人不会不惜任何代价订阅。“订阅的重复性提供了一个主要的心理障碍，让他们很难承诺。
但是，您可能只有一小部分用户可以从您的应用程序中获得最大价值，并且愿意订阅。这些都是你最忠实、最铁杆的用户。当你设计你的订阅计划时，你想要关注这些用户，他们是那些愿意付钱并留下来的人(为什么这很重要，请参阅我关于客户流失的帖子)。通过订阅，您可以选择专注于更少的客户，而不是最忠诚的客户。考虑到这是一个小得多的客户群，你需要收取更高的价格。
订阅存在这样的心理障碍，一旦有人愿意购买，他们很可能对价格不那么敏感。如果有人愿意以1.99美元/月的价格购买，他们很可能会以4.99美元/月甚至7.99美元/月的价格购买。我们在推出Astropad Studio之前进行的调查也显示了这一点：
这是对一款新应用的潜在定价方案进行测试的真实数据。你会注意到的第一件事是，人们更愿意一次性购买。
其次，如果我们将愿意购买20美元/年订阅的人数与准备购买100美元/年订阅的人数进行比较，你会注意到愿意支付100美元/年的人数减少了2.5倍。但是，虽然我们每年100美元的人数减少了2.5倍，但我们的价格却上涨了5倍！看一下每万名游客收入的估计也清楚地表明，我们每年100美元的收入将是只有20美元/年的收入的两倍！
因此，如果你打算转向订阅(我会再写一篇文章，解释为什么我认为这是生产力应用的正确选择)，你应该专注于你最好的客户，并向他们收取更高的费用。你不仅会产生更多的收入，从而实现增长，而且以更高的价格购买的客户往往会减少流失(我们知道流失是如何成为杀手的)。所以提高您的订阅价格吧！
note_en:
The conventional wisdom for app subscriptions is to price affordably, like $1.99/month, and hopefully attract lots of subscribers at a low price. Then by having low pricing, fewer will reject the idea of subscriptions, and you’ll make up the difference with volume. But this is the wrong approach…
  The reality is most people won’t sign up for your subscription even at a low price. Most people dislike subscriptions, and many won’t subscribe at any cost. The recurring nature of subscriptions provides a major mental hurdle and makes them hard to commit to.
 However, you likely have a smaller set of users who get the most value out of your app and are willing to subscribe. These are your most devoted and die-hard users. When designing your subscription plan you want to focus on these users, they are the ones who will pay and stick around (see my  post on churn for why this is critical).  By going subscription, you are choosing to focus on fewer customers but your most dedicated customers. Given that this is a much smaller customer base, you need to charge a higher price.
 There is such a mental barrier with subscriptions that once someone is willing to purchase, they are likely less price sensitive. If someone is willing to buy at $1.99/month, they are very likely to buy at $4.99/month or even $7.99/month. Our surveys conducted before launching Astropad Studio show this as well:
   This is real data from a test on potential pricing schemes for a new app. The first thing you’ll notice is that people are much more willing to make a one-time purchase.
 Second, if we compare the number of people willing to purchase a $20/year subscription vs. the number of people ready to buy a $100/year subscription, you’ll notice that 2.5x fewer people are willing to pay $100/year . But while we have 2.5x fewer people at $100/year, our price has gone up by 5x! Looking at the estimates for revenue per 10,000 visitors clearly shows as well,  we would make 2x as much revenue at $100/year than we would at just $20/year!
 So if you’re going to make the switch to subscription (and I’ll be writing another post on why I think it is the right choice for productivity apps) you should focus on your best customers and charge them more. Not only will you generate more revenue with which to grow, but customers that purchase at higher prices tend to churn less (and we know  how churn can be a killer). So raise your subscription prices!
title: 应用订阅收费更高

如果你认为设计微处理器很复杂，试着看看它的所有权传奇。上周，围绕MIPS微处理器家族的许可权、所有权、版税和政治的错综复杂的轨迹又出现了奇怪的转折，结果MIPS现在掌握在中国人手中。说大也大吧。
据路透社报道，上海的一家名为CIP United Co.Ltd.的公司刚刚购买了MIPS处理器架构的全部合法许可权，“为中国、香港和澳门的所有新客户和现有客户，以及开发基于MIPS架构的新衍生技术的能力”。CIP可能并不拥有MIPS公司的全部股份，但它现在确实管理着东亚的一大块地区。据路透社消息人士称，同样重要的是，CIP可以开发自己的MIPS处理器。“整个中国大陆的许可证已经卖完了，货真价实。”
这正是美国政府在干预之前对MIPS Technologies的收购时试图避免的。然而，这些努力似乎在最终登陆中国之前，通过与离岸控股公司的一系列复杂交易而受挫。
在MIPS官方网站上查看，仍然显示它是一家位于加利福尼亚州圣克拉拉的公司。这是它多年来一直占据的位置。从这个意义上说，它几乎没有移动，尽管情况发生了很大的变化。奇怪的是，该网站对新的许可协议只字不提。事实上，在撰写本文时，该网站的新闻链接已中断。
MIPS最近的所有者是/现在是Wave Computing，一家现已破产的人工智能初创公司，也位于圣克拉拉。当时，Wave似乎不太可能成为收购者，更像是被许可人，而不是所有者。在Wave之前，有熵研究实验室(Entropy Research Labs)，这是一家短暂的控股公司，所有权先于同样神秘的塔尔伍德风险投资公司(Tallwood Venture Capital)。并非巧合的是，这三家公司都是由同一个人拥有和/或创立的，一个名叫Diosdado“Dado”Banatao的人，他出生于菲律宾，是一位成功的工程师，老前辈可能记得他在20世纪80年代是Chip&amp；Technologies和S3 Graphics的联合创始人。
在此之前四年，MIPS是英国IP巨头想象力技术公司(Imagination Technologies)的一部分，后者在MIPS还是一家独立公司的时候，曾斥资1亿美元从MIPS手中收购了MIPS。但在苹果公开宣布将不再使用Imagination的PowerVR显卡后，Imagination的股价暴跌，整个公司被Canyon Bridge收购。嗯，几乎是整个公司。由于Canyon Bridge主要由中国人拥有，想象力被迫从MIPS剥离出来，单独出售，这就是它最终出现在塔尔伍德的原因。
在想象力被收购之前，MIPS Technologies是自己的公司，是从Silicon Graphics(SGI)剥离出来的，而SGI在1992年又吸收了MIPS计算机系统公司。困惑了吗？这里有一张很好的图片，记录了多年来MIPS的所有权。如有更改，恕不另行通知。
据推测，CIP在中国、香港和澳门领土之外的客户仍然可以授权加州Wave Computing的MIPS处理器设计。但即便如此，这也不是直截了当的。WAVE的许可活动是由一家在开曼群岛注册的控股公司MIPS Technologies International Ltd处理的。现在很难再知道把版税支票寄到哪里了。
目前也不清楚Wave是否仍然可以合法地开发新的MIPS处理器，或者这是否是CIP的唯一权限。这个问题可能没有意义。Wave于4月份根据破产法第11章申请破产保护，除了继续开灯外，该公司几乎没有多余的工程人才了。就在18个月前，Wave的身价为6亿美元，雇佣了250名员工。现在它大约有24个，基本上已经一文不值了。出售其最大的资产-MIPS-是其偿付能力的最后希望。
对MIPS来说，这是一次漫长而奇怪的旅行。最初是一个大学研究项目(在大卫·帕特森博士的领导下)，然后是一家超级计算机公司，然后是一个获得许可的CPU核心，然后是一个嵌入式处理器，现在已经成为历史的注脚。有一段时间，MIPS和任何东西一样快速、创新和令人兴奋。它将在自己的游戏中击败英特尔。然后，它将挑战ARM。它为索尼和任天堂视频游戏提供了动力。它呈现了“侏罗纪公园”的电影。它竭尽全力，但不知何故在每一个转折点都输了，这是硅谷成功的不幸反例。它曲折的旅程现在继续在世界的另一边。
note_en:
If you think designing a microprocessor is complicated, try following its ownership saga. The tangled trail of licensing rights, ownership, royalties, and politics surrounding the MIPS microprocessor family took another weird turn last week, with the result that MIPS is now in Chinese hands. Sort of.
 As the   Reuters news agency reports , a Shanghai-based company called CIP United Co. Ltd. just bought the full legal rights to license the MIPS processor architecture “for all new and existing customers in China, Hong Kong and Macau, as well as the ability to develop new derivative technologies based on the MIPS architecture.” CIP may not own the entirety of MIPS, the company, but it does now manage a big chunk of East Asia. Just as important, CIP can develop its own MIPS processors, according to Reuters’ sources. “A license for all of mainland China has already been sold, lock, stock and barrel.”
 This is precisely what the U.S. government was trying to avoid when it intervened in the previous acquisition of MIPS Technologies. However, it appears those efforts were foiled through a series of complex transactions with offshore holding companies before finally landing in China.
  Checking in on the   official MIPS website  still shows it as a Santa Clara, California company. That’s the same location it has occupied for years. In that sense, it’s barely moved, though the situation is much altered. Oddly, the site says nothing about the new licensing deal. In fact, the site’s   news link was broken  as of this writing.
 MIPS’s most recent owner was/is   Wave Computing , a now-bankrupt AI startup also based in Santa Clara. Wave seemed like an unlikely acquirer at the time, more of a licensee than an owner. Before Wave there was Entropy Research Labs, a short-lived holding company that was preceded in ownership by the equally mysterious Tallwood Venture Capital. Not coincidentally, all three firms were owned and/or founded by the same person, one Diosdado “Dado” Banatao, a successful Philippines-born engineer whom old-timers may remember as the co-founder of both Chips & Technologies and S3 Graphics in the 1980s.
 Four years before all of that, MIPS was part of UK-based IP powerhouse Imagination Technologies, which had paid $100 million to acquire MIPS from, well, MIPS, back when it was a standalone company. But when Imagination’s stock tanked following Apple’s public announcement that it would no longer use Imagination’s PowerVR graphics, the entire company was acquired by Canyon Bridge. Well, almost the entire company. Because Canyon Bridge is largely Chinese-owned, Imagination was forced to split off MIPS and sell it separately, which is how it wound up at Tallwood.
 Before Imagination’s ownership, MIPS Technologies was its own company, having been spun out of Silicon Graphics (SGI), which had, in turn, absorbed MIPS Computer Systems in 1992. Confused yet? There’s a nice graphic chronicling MIPS ownership through the years   here . Subject to change without notice.
  Presumably, customers outside of CIP’s territory in China, Hong Kong, and Macau can still license MIPS processor designs from Wave Computing in California. But even that isn’t straightforward. Wave’s licensing activity is handled by a holding company registered in the Cayman Islands called MIPS Technologies International Ltd. It’s hard to know where to send the royalty checks anymore.
 It’s also not clear whether Wave can still legally develop new MIPS processors, or if that’s the sole purview of CIP. The question may be moot.   Wave filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in April  and the company has little engineering talent left to spare beyond keeping the lights on. Just 18 months ago, Wave was worth $600 million and employed 250 people. Now it has about two dozen and is essentially valueless. Selling off its biggest asset – MIPS – was its last hope for solvency.
 It’s been a long, strange trip for MIPS. What started out as a university research project (under Dr. David Patterson, no less), then a supercomputer company, then a licensed CPU core, then an embedded processor, is now an historical footnote. For a while, MIPS was as fast and innovative and exciting as anything. It was going to beat Intel at its own game. Then it was going to challenge ARM. It powered Sony and Nintendo video games. It rendered   Jurassic Park  movies. It had everything going for it, and yet somehow lost at every turn, the unlucky counterexample of Silicon Valley success. Its convoluted journey now continues on the opposite side of the world.
title: 松动嘴唇下沉MIPS

Libtorrent-2.0刚刚发布，有几个主要的新特性。其中之一是对BitTorrent v2的支持。
BEP 52的大部分规范工作是由8472完成的。对BitTorrent v2的libtorrent支持主要是由Steven Siloti实现的。BiglyBT还将在不久的将来发布BitTorrent v2的实现。
在谷歌宣布产生冲突后不久，BitTorrent v2开始努力摆脱SHA-1作为碎片的散列函数。考虑到一个新的散列函数不会向后兼容，在我们遭受兼容性打击的同时，还提出了一些其他的更改。这篇文章描述了BitTorrent v2协议的新特性。
片断数据的散列函数更改为SHA-256。这样做的一个结果是散列是32字节而不是20字节。在BitTorrent v2中，信息字典也是由SHA-256计算的，这对DHT和跟踪器的兼容性构成了挑战，后者的协议要求20字节的哈希。为了处理此问题，分布式哈希表和跟踪器对v2 Torrent的通告和查找使用截断为20字节的SHA-256信息散列。
这是最初创建v2协议的基本原理之一。这意味着从根本上说，v2 Torrent将由与v1 Torrent不同的散列来标识，这将始终创建单独的群，即使在共享相同的文件时也是如此。稍后将在向后兼容性一节中详细介绍这一点。
在BitTorrent v1中，片段是散列的，生成的散列包含在.torrent文件/元数据中(在信息字典中)。在大多数情况下，片段散列是.Torrent文件大小的大部分。要将.torrent文件大小保持在大文件的合理大小范围内，可以增加片段大小，这意味着每个哈希表示文件的更大部分。较大片段大小的结果是，如果散列失败，则必须重新下载文件的较大部分，直到该片段通过散列检查。
改进这两个度量的一个旧想法是使用Merkle散列树来表示片段散列(最初是在Tribler中实现的)。这会使.torrent文件保持较小，因为您只需要树的根哈希。BitTorrent v2对片段使用Merkle散列树(但Tribler实现的协议不同)。这有以下优点：
Torrent元数据(特别是.Torrent文件的信息字典部分)变得小得多。这减少了添加磁铁链接时的启动延迟，因为在Torrent下载本身可以开始之前需要下载更少的字节。
下载的数据可以在块级别上进行验证。这意味着如果对等设备发送损坏的数据，它可以立即被发现，并且只需要重新下载16 KiB。也可以确定地识别发送损坏数据的对等体。与识别v1中的坏对等点(有时称为智能禁令)所需的启发式算法相比，这是一个很大的改进。
散列树的叶子始终为16 KiB(数据块大小)，而不考虑数据段大小。片段大小的概念仍然存在，并且仍然在对等线路协议中使用，就像今天一样。例如在HAVE和REQUEST消息中。但是，片段散列实际上不是片段内容的散列，而是片段散列树的根。即完整Merkle树的子树。
在v2中，.torrent文件必须仍然包含这些片段散列(实际上是Merkle树中代表片段级别的散列)。这有助于分发和存储散列，以便在重新启动正在设定种子的客户端时不必重新计算它们。它们还存储在恢复状态中。对于v2 Torrent，.torrent文件大小并不小，因为它仍然包含片段散列，但是信息字典是小的，这是磁铁链接开始下载所需的部分。
BitTorrent v2不仅使用哈希树，还为Torrent中的每个文件形成哈希树。这有几个优点：
相同的文件将始终具有相同的哈希，并且可以更容易地从一个Torrent移动到另一个Torrent(在创建Torrent时)，而不必重新对任何内容进行哈希处理。相同的文件也可以更容易地跨不同的群进行识别，因为它们的根哈希仅取决于文件的内容。
所有文件都将分片对齐，这意味着每个文件后面都有隐式PAD文件。
这解决了一个长期的愿望，即更容易地跨群识别重复文件或查找文件的多个源。
正如前面提到的，在大多数情况下，片段散列占据了信息字典中的大部分空间。例外情况是具有大量小文件的Torrent；然后是使用最多空间的文件列表。在v1 Torrents中，文件列表表示为具有完整路径的单个文件列表。在具有深层目录结构的Torrent中(或仅具有长名称的目录)，这些目录名将重复多次，从而加剧问题。
V2 Torrent通过对目录结构使用更有效的编码来解决这个问题，减少了重复。目录结构不是平面列表，而是存储为树(使用本码字典)。这导致目录名只被提及一次。例如：
